messages.content: Always use code blocks with the appropriate language tags. If asked for a table always format it using Markdown syntax.
how to get diff between two commits using git in cli

To get the diff between two commits using Git in the command line interface, you can use the `git diff` command followed by the commit hashes you want to compare. Here is the syntax:

```bash
git diff <commit-hash-1> <commit-hash-2>
```

Replace `<commit-hash-1>` and `<commit-hash-2>` with the actual commit hashes you want to compare.

If you want to see the changes introduced by each commit individually, you can also use the `git show` command with the respective commit hashes:

```bash
git show <commit-hash-1>
git show <commit-hash-2>
```

This will show the changes introduced by each commit separately.

If you want to see the changes between the two commits in a more compact format, you can use the `git log` command with the `..` notation:

```bash
git log <commit-hash-1>..<commit-hash-2>
```

This will show the commit messages and metadata between the two specified commits.

Remember, you can also use branch names instead of commit hashes in these commands.
how to go back after checking out to a commit

If you want to go back to your latest commit after checking out to a previous commit in Git, you can simply use the following command:

```bash
git checkout -
```

This command will switch back to the most recent commit on the branch you were on before checking out to the previous commit.

Alternatively, if you have switched to a different commit and want to go back to your current branch's `HEAD` (latest commit on the branch), you can use the following command:

```bash
git checkout <branch-name>
```

Replace `<branch-name>` with the name of your branch.

In case you want to undo the checkout of a specific commit and bring your repository back to its previous state before the checkout, you can use the reflog to find the reference to your previous state and checkout that reference. Here's how you can do it:

1. Find the commit hash or reference you want to go back to using the reflog:

```bash
git reflog
```

2. Copy the commit hash or reference that represents the previous state you want to return to.

3. Check out that commit hash or reference:

```bash
git checkout <commit-hash-or-reference>
```

By following these steps, you can easily navigate back to a specific commit or revert back to your latest commit after checking out to a previous commit.

OSC_noSteamGateway(std::string portTargetOSC = "9000", std::string ipTargetOSC = "192.168.1.4"); i wanna use this constructor would it work like this  OSC_noSteamGateway(); or will it call the default ?

If you have a constructor in C++ with default parameter values, like the `OSC_noSteamGateway` constructor you provided:

```cpp
OSC_noSteamGateway(std::string portTargetOSC = "9000", std::string ipTargetOSC = "192.168.1.4");
```

You can create an object of the class `OSC_noSteamGateway` without passing any arguments and it will use the default parameter values specified in the constructor. So, the following code will call the constructor with default values:

```cpp
OSC_noSteamGateway gateway;
```

The default constructor (`OSC_noSteamGateway();`) does not need to be defined explicitly because your constructor with default parameters serves as both a parameterized constructor and a default constructor.

In summary, when you create an object of the `OSC_noSteamGateway` class without passing any arguments, it will effectively use the default values specified in the constructor declaration.
